author: @reypolice5231
text:
Butcher paper that they wrap meat with. If you go down to the butcher shop. They will give you some if you ask.

It's stronger than news paper.

author: @reypolice5231
text:
How would spraying glue on each sheet in the stack, before rolling it up improve the strength?

Would crisscrossing the sheets like plywood and gluing them like plywood make it stronger?

I saw on a video by that stick maker that he said: " if you want it really strong you, you use 6 sheets"

You mentioned you are only using 5 sheets. How much heavier could it be with 6 sheets? Not much I think. Divide ➗ 225 grams = 45 grams + 225= 270 grams. Add spray glue and it's a little bit more? Still less than steel but more strength as plywood type build maybe?

text: Adding suspension to the bike would be a good idea to avoid the hard jolts of uneven roads. If you use the widest tyres you can fit on the rims and a low pressure that would give some level of pneumatic suspension

replies.author: @YeshuaGod22
replies.text: Given how bendy and springy the tubes are, I get the impression the back fork is effectively providing shock absorbtion already. Lowering tyre pressure would increase battery load and reduce the rate of forward acceleration.

author: @YeshuaGod22
text:
Fascinating as always!
Would love to see paper bike mk1 tested thoroughly with the 500W motor to failure before mk2 gets built! Would it handle a slalom well or would there be excessive torsion across the length of the bike, suggesting a couple of layers of cross- ply or diagonal ply may be beneficial or adjusting the thickness of the tubes? So many questions that 120W can't answer lol!
The stixx does an impressive job of storing and releasing energy in today's vid. Pretty sure if a metal or carbon frame deformed that much it would have no chance of returning to straight. That property has interesting implications! Would also be cool to see what sort of load a stixx can withstand in compression, torsion and tension.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think the mark 1 held up just fine but for torture testing I think mark 2 would be better

author: @DarkByte
text:
perhaps you could use a hanging scale attached to the center of a stick and a bucket below to measure how much it will endure by adding weight to the bucket until it snaps. some scales also come with a function to hold the maximum measured weight, which could be used to measure the dynamic load capacity.
 would also be interesting to know how much one of those paper bikes weighs compared to a steel one.

author: @egmontnicolas2160

author: @leostarling5746
text: I do not think that the loosely rolled up paper inside the tube is "carrying its weight" in terms of its strength contribution. You might be better off with extra glued layers up to the same weight.  Unless you want specifically longitudinal tensile strength (like in a rope). That bamboo you worked with before knows it. You would have trouble breaking a bamboo stick of similar weight. Unless you eat a lot more. You can get the strongest/lightest structure if you analyse the pushing/pulling/bending forces and use specific materials for them. For the bicycle, that means bamboo for the top bars (pushing, bending) and thin steel cable to replace  the bottom bars of the frame (stretching). The kind of cable used for bicycle brakes would carry your weight easily and is very light.

replies.author: @YeshuaGod22
replies.text: The front triangle also needs to resist torsion, so any weight savings from using a cable for a downtube would likely be more than offset by needing to massively increase the torsional load strength of the top tube

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: @YeshuaGod22 I don't think that the steering torsion is significant, at least not on a bike. If you try to manhandle a bike round the bend by  forceful steering, rather than by leaning in, you will come off!

author: @barbufodor1186
text: as far as recycling goes this is a phenomenal use. Any kind of decent structure can be build out of it: shelves, stairs, ladders, furniture, supports of any kind etc. I am thinking that the resistance to compression and elongation must be also decent.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I kind of expected shear to be low - I am with you in compression and extension I think it would be much more impressive

author: @michaelwarbon
text: Another idea.  Put a tiny amount of nanocellose/gell between each layer.  It would make a super strong stick with unbelievable characteristics of nanocellose.  Each layer of paper would actually help the nanocellose to align properly.  When they compressed wood they made it stronger than steel.  I think this layered nanocellose approach could rival it (you may need to do it in the presence of a magnet).

author: @danrawson8515

Hi Robert I love Your Content
you really have got me hooked and got me thinking about this project below.. 
Do you know of projects that use water to get energy?
 I have a thought big tanks full of water,  in a circle using syphons  to draw the water to the Next tank and the same with each tank and having turbines in all  the pipes when the water falls into the next tank it produces energy
and a ram pump to get the water back into the empty tanks and theoretically it Should keep running. So could be Free energy..
Would Love your thought on this idea.. thank-you for making me think More..

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
Composites are fantastic materials! 
Add a single sheet of rayon or other cellulose-based fabric -- even cheap synthetic chamois -- and I'd bet you'd near double your stixx strength in that stress orientation. Discarded cloth, like linen or cotton, would do nearly as well. Or even add a single, diagonally oriented sheet of kraft paper (don't know what you call dark brown hard packing paper in the U.K.) would increase the strength.  
Enjoyed the test, great video!

author: @OverlandOne
text: You could jump up and down once on a bathroom scale like you just did on the Stixx tube and, using a camera, capture the highest reading it shows to get and idea of how much you loaded the tube when it failed.  Just simply duplicate your jump height over the scale as it was over the tube.  You can use this video you made here to get an idea of that distance.  This, of course, will not be exact in any way but will give a rough idea.  My guess is that it took at least 3 times your weight to cause a failure.  I think F=MA will apply here.
